title: Getting Started with Open Source: A Beginner's Guide
author: Taylor Lee
content:
Contributing to open source can seem intimidating at first, but it's one of the best ways to improve your coding skills, learn from experienced developers, and give back to the community. This guide will walk you through the basics of getting started with open source contributions.

Why Contribute to Open Source?

Open source contributions offer numerous benefits:

  • Real-world experience: Work on production codebases used by thousands or millions of users
  • Learn from others: Read code written by experienced developers and receive feedback on your contributions
  • Build your portfolio: Showcase your work and demonstrate your skills to potential employers
  • Give back: Help improve tools and projects you use every day
  • Network: Connect with developers from around the world

Understanding the Basics

Before diving in, make sure you're comfortable with:

Version Control with Git

Git is essential for open source contribution. You should understand:

  • Cloning repositories
  • Creating branches
  • Making commits
  • Pushing and pulling changes
  • Resolving merge conflicts

GitHub Workflow

Most open source projects use GitHub. Familiarize yourself with:

  • Forking repositories
  • Creating pull requests
  • Writing good commit messages
  • Using issues and discussions

Finding Your First Project

Start with projects that:

  • Interest you: Choose something you use or care about
  • Welcome beginners: Look for tags like "good first issue" or "beginner-friendly"
  • Have active maintainers: Check if issues and pull requests receive responses
  • Provide documentation: Good projects have clear contribution guidelines

Some great places to find beginner-friendly projects:

  • GitHub's "Explore" section
  • First Timers Only
  • Good First Issue
  • Up For Grabs

Making Your First Contribution

Here's a step-by-step process:

1. Read the Documentation

Before contributing, read:

  • README.md
  • CONTRIBUTING.md
  • CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md
  • Open and closed issues

2. Start Small

Don't try to rewrite the entire codebase. Good first contributions include:

  • Fixing typos in documentation
  • Adding code comments
  • Writing tests
  • Fixing small bugs
  • Improving error messages

3. Communicate

  • Comment on the issue you want to work on
  • Ask questions if something is unclear
  • Keep maintainers updated on your progress

4. Write Quality Code

  • Follow the project's coding style
  • Write clear, descriptive commit messages
  • Add tests if applicable
  • Update documentation if needed

5. Create a Pull Request

When creating your PR:

  • Reference the related issue
  • Describe what you changed and why
  • Include screenshots for UI changes
  • Be patient and responsive to feedback

Common Mistakes to Avoid

  • Not reading the guidelines: Every project has its own process
  • Taking on too much: Start small and build confidence
  • Being defensive: Accept feedback gracefully and learn from it
  • Abandoning your PR: If you can't finish, communicate that to maintainers
  • Ignoring coding standards: Follow the project's style guide

Dealing with Rejection

Not every contribution will be accepted, and that's okay. Common reasons include:

  • The change doesn't align with project goals
  • The timing isn't right
  • The approach needs refinement

Learn from the feedback, don't take it personally, and keep contributing.

Building a Habit

Make open source contribution a regular practice:

  • Set aside time each week
  • Track your contributions
  • Celebrate small wins
  • Connect with other contributors

Conclusion

Starting with open source can be challenging, but it's incredibly rewarding. Begin with small contributions, learn continuously, and don't be afraid to ask questions. Every experienced open source contributor was once a beginner too.

The open source community welcomes you. Happy contributing!

Have questions about getting started with open source? Reach out to us at our next workshop or on our Discord server.

title: Fall Hackathon 2024: Innovation, Collaboration, and Amazing Projects
author: Morgan Rodriguez
content:
Our Fall Hackathon 2024 was an incredible success! Over the weekend of October 12-13, 80+ students came together for 48 hours of innovation, collaboration, and creativity. Here's a recap of this amazing event.

Event Overview

The Fall Hackathon brought together students from all years and majors to build innovative projects addressing real-world problems. Participants formed teams, brainstormed ideas, and coded their way through two intense days of development.

By the Numbers

  • 80+ participants from various departments
  • 22 project submissions across multiple categories
  • 15 mentors from industry and academia
  • 8 sponsors providing resources and prizes
  • Countless cups of coffee consumed

Categories and Themes

Teams could choose from several categories:

Web Applications: Build full-stack web apps solving everyday problems
Mobile Development: Create Android or iOS applications
AI/ML Projects: Leverage artificial intelligence and machine learning
Hardware/IoT: Integrate hardware with software solutions
Social Good: Projects addressing social or environmental challenges

Winning Projects

First Place: EcoTrack

A mobile application that gamifies sustainable living by tracking carbon footprint and suggesting eco-friendly alternatives.

Second Place: StudyBuddy AI

An AI-powered study companion that generates personalized quizzes, summarizes lecture content, and adapts to individual learning styles.

Third Place: AccessiWeb

A browser extension that automatically enhances website accessibility by adjusting contrast, font sizes, and adding screen reader improvements.

Special Awards

  • Best UI/UX: LocalConnect - A platform connecting residents with local businesses
  • Most Innovative Use of Technology: HealthPredict - ML-based early disease detection
  • Best First-Time Hackers: QuickRecipe - Recipe discovery based on available ingredients
  • People's Choice: CampusNav - AR-based campus navigation for new students

Highlights and Memorable Moments

Opening Ceremony

The event kicked off Friday evening with inspiring talks from our sponsors and alumni who shared their startup journeys.

Midnight Pizza Party

No hackathon is complete without late-night fuel!

Final Presentations

Sunday afternoon, teams showcased their projects to judges and attendees.

Thank You

This event wouldn't have been possible without our sponsors, mentors, volunteers, and participants.

What's Next

Based on feedback, we're already planning improvements for our Spring Hackathon.

Interested in participating in our next hackathon? Join our mailing list and follow us on social media for updates!

title: Modern Web Development Best Practices for 2025
author: Sam Chen
content:
Web development evolves rapidly, and keeping up with best practices is essential for building maintainable, performant, and secure applications.

Frontend Best Practices

1. Mobile-First Responsive Design

Always design for mobile devices first, then scale up to larger screens.

2. Semantic HTML

Use appropriate HTML elements for better accessibility and SEO.

3. Performance Optimization

  • Minimize HTTP requests: Bundle and minify CSS/JS files
  • Lazy load images: Load images only when they enter viewport
  • Use CDNs: Serve static assets from content delivery networks
  • Optimize images: Use modern formats like WebP

4. Accessibility

Make your websites usable for everyone with proper alt text, heading hierarchy, keyboard navigation, and color contrast.

Backend Best Practices

1. RESTful API Design

Follow REST principles for consistent, predictable APIs.

2. Security

Use HTTPS, validate input, sanitize data, use parameterized queries, implement rate limiting, and keep dependencies updated.

3. Error Handling

Provide meaningful error messages without exposing sensitive information.

Development Workflow

Version Control

Use Git effectively with descriptive commit messages, feature branches, and pull requests.

Code Quality

Follow style guides, write tests, do code reviews, use linters, and document code.

Conclusion

Web development best practices ensure your applications are maintainable, performant, and secure.

Want to learn more about web development? Join our upcoming workshops!

title: Semiconductors
author: Horizon
content:
Semiconductors lie at the heart of modern technology. From smartphones and computers to solar panels and satellites, nearly every modern electronic device relies on semiconductor materials. Yet the process used to manufacture these crystals on Earth is fundamentally limited by physics — particularly by convection driven by gravity.

Although engineers have developed techniques to mitigate these effects, a more radical idea has emerged: why not manufacture semiconductors in space?

A Factory in Orbit

In 2025, the company Space Forge took a step toward this goal by launching ForgeStar-1, a satellite carrying a miniature remotely operable furnace. The system was activated in December, reaching temperatures of nearly 1000 °C and generating plasma.

The mission served as a proof-of-concept experiment demonstrating that semiconductor manufacturing processes can operate in orbit.

Why Leave the Planet?

The primary motivation for producing semiconductors in orbit is the presence of microgravity. On Earth, buoyancy causes hot fluid to rise while colder fluid sinks. These motions create convection currents within molten semiconductor material.

These currents transport impurities and dopants unevenly through the melt, producing crystals with non-uniform properties.

Microgravity and Its Advantages

In orbit, the effective value of g in the Rayleigh number becomes extremely small. As a result, buoyancy-driven convection is greatly suppressed.

Crystal growth then occurs primarily through diffusion, producing significantly more uniform materials.

Space Forge claims that this environment could allow semiconductors to be produced that are up to 4000 times purer than those grown on Earth.

Remaining Challenges

Despite the advantages of microgravity, crystal growth in space is not completely free from fluid motion. One important mechanism is surface-tension-driven convection, also known as the Marangoni effect.

Another major obstacle is economic rather than physical: launching and retrieving material from orbit remains extremely expensive.

The Next Steps

While ForgeStar-1 was primarily a demonstration mission and will not return to Earth, the follow-up mission ForgeStar-2 aims to demonstrate the safe recovery of materials manufactured in orbit.

If successful, this technology could eventually lead to orbital manufacturing facilities, where reusable satellites act as miniature factories producing ultra-high-purity semiconductor materials.
